title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana dashboard — the consistent daily memory climb is a really helpful data point.

To help narrow down where the memory is going: when you see the 4GB usage, is that the TaskFlow application process itself (e.g., the Node/Java/Python process), or the overall server memory? If you're able to check, does the database process (e.g., PostgreSQL) also show growing memory usage, or does it stay stable while the app process climbs? Also, which version of TaskFlow are you running, and how is it deployed (Docker, bare metal, etc.)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It's the TaskFlow application process itself that's climbing. I've been watching it with `htop` and the TaskFlow process steadily grows while PostgreSQL stays pretty stable around 300-400MB the whole day. We're running TaskFlow v2.3 — we actually just upgraded from v2.2 about a week ago, which is right around when this started happening now that I think about it. It's deployed bare metal on an Ubuntu 22.04 VM with 8GB RAM.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really useful detail — the timing lining up with the v2.3 upgrade is a strong clue.

One thing that would help us narrow this down further: does the memory growth seem tied to user activity, or does it happen even during quiet periods? For example, over a weekend or outside business hours when fewer people are using it, does the memory still climb at the same rate, or does it slow down or plateau? If you haven't had a chance to observe that yet, even a rough sense of whether the growth accelerates during peak usage hours vs. stays steady would be helpful.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question — it's definitely tied to user activity. Over the weekend when almost nobody is using it, the memory barely moves at all. During the workday it climbs steadily, and it's noticeably faster during peak hours when everyone's active. I'd estimate it grows maybe 10MB per hour or so based on what I see in Grafana, but it seems to snowball later in the day — like the growth accelerates. By the end of an 8-hour workday we're up around 3-4GB. The v2.3 changelog mentioned something about 'improved real-time notifications' as one of the new features, which I wonder about since that's the kind of thing that would be active whenever users are online.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
The timing with v2.3 and the real-time notifications feature is suspicious — that's worth investigating directly. Does TaskFlow v2.3 have a way to disable the real-time notifications feature (a config flag, environment variable, etc.)? If so, it would be really valuable to try running with it disabled for a day to see if the memory growth stops.

Alternatively, if you can check: when the memory is high late in the day, does the number of active WebSocket connections (or SSE connections) shown in your monitoring match the number of users actually online at that moment, or is it significantly higher? That would tell us whether notification connections are accumulating rather than being cleaned up when users leave.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I haven't found a config flag to disable real-time notifications — I'll dig through the docs and config files more carefully, but nothing jumped out at me. I can try that if I find one though, that would be a good test.

As for WebSocket connections, that's actually interesting. I do have some Grafana metrics on WebSocket connections, and the active connection count looks roughly in line with how many users are online — it goes up during the day and drops off in the evening, which seems normal. But I did notice that the server seems to be doing a LOT more work per connection than I'd expect. CPU spikes correlate with the memory growth too. So it doesn't look like connections are piling up exactly, but something related to those connections seems to be accumulating internally. I wish I had more granular metrics on what's happening inside the notification system specifically.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a great observation — connections looking normal but internal work snowballing is a classic sign of something accumulating in memory per-request or per-event without being cleaned up.

To help developers reproduce this in a test environment: have you noticed whether the memory growth happens even with just a handful of users (say, 1-3 people), or does it only become noticeable at scale with many concurrent users? And when people are using TaskFlow during the day, is it mostly a mix of everything (creating tasks, commenting, moving items between boards, etc.), or is there a particular workflow that dominates — like heavy use of a specific feature or page that stays open all day?

If the leak shows up even with a few users doing normal operations, that would make it much easier to track down.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I haven't really had a chance to test with just a few users since it's a production server and people are always on it during work hours. But based on the growth rate I see — roughly 10MB/hour with 200 users active — I'd guess with only 2-3 users it would be so slow you might not even notice it over a workday. It seems proportional to the number of people using it.

As for workflows, it's a pretty typical mix of everything — creating and updating tasks, commenting, dragging things between boards, viewing dashboards. Nothing unusual or dominated by one feature. Most people have TaskFlow open in a browser tab throughout the day and interact with it on and off. I don't think any particular action triggers it more than others; it just seems like normal usage across the board causes the steady climb.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in TaskFlow v2.3 application process causes progressive slowdown requiring daily restart","problem":"The TaskFlow application process exhibits a memory leak introduced in v2.3, growing from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over an 8-hour workday with ~200 active users. This causes page loads to exceed 10 seconds and API timeouts by late afternoon, requiring a daily server restart. The leak is proportional to user activity and does not occur during idle periods.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The v2.3 'improved real-time notifications' feature is accumulating internal state (event listeners, notification history buffers, or per-connection metadata) that is not being cleaned up when events are processed or users disconnect. WebSocket connection counts appear normal, but per-connection CPU and memory work snowballs over time, suggesting a growing internal data structure that is iterated or appended to on each notification event without pruning. The accelerating growth pattern (faster later in the day) points to an O(n²) or similar superlinear cost — e.g., broadcasting to a listener list that grows but is never trimmed, or an in-memory event log that is scanned on each new event.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a test environment (Ubuntu 22.04, PostgreSQL, bare metal or VM with 8GB RAM)","Simulate 20-50 concurrent users performing mixed operations (create/update tasks, comment, drag between boards, view dashboards) using a load testing tool","Ensure users have TaskFlow open in long-lived sessions (simulating browser tabs open throughout the day)","Monitor application process memory via htop or process metrics over several hours","Observe memory climbing proportionally to user count and time, with growth accelerating","Compare by deploying v2.2 with the same load — memory should remain stable"],"environment":"TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2 approximately one week ago), deployed bare metal on Ubuntu 22.04 VM with 8GB RAM, PostgreSQL database (stable at 300-400MB), approximately 200 active users during business hours","severity":"high","impact":"All 200 active users experience progressive degradation throughout the workday. By afternoon, the application becomes nearly unusable (10+ second page loads, API timeouts). Operations team must restart the server daily. This is a regression from v2.2 which did not exhibit this behavior.","recommended_fix":"1. Take a heap dump of the running process late in the day when memory is high and diff against a fresh-start heap to identify the accumulating objects. 2. Audit the v2.3 real-time notifications code path — specifically look for: (a) event listener registrations that are not removed on disconnect or page navigation, (b) in-memory notification history or event buffers that grow unbounded, (c) per-user or per-session caches in the notification system that are never evicted. 3. Compare the notification system implementation between v2.2 and v2.3 to identify what changed. 4. As a short-term mitigation, investigate whether real-time notifications can be disabled via configuration or the notification subsystem can be restarted independently without a full server restart.","proposed_test_case":"Create an integration test that simulates 50 users connecting, performing mixed task operations over a simulated 2-hour period (compressed time with high event throughput), then verifies that application heap usage remains within a bounded range (e.g., no more than 2× baseline). Users should connect and disconnect throughout the test to verify connection cleanup. The test should fail if memory grows linearly with total events processed rather than stabilizing proportional to active connections.","information_gaps":["No heap dump or profiling data available to confirm exactly which objects are accumulating","Exact v2.3 changelog diff for the notification system has not been reviewed","Unknown whether a configuration flag exists to disable real-time notifications (reporter could not find one)","No confirmation of whether a v2.2 rollback resolves the issue (reporter has not attempted this)"]}
